seoTitle: 3peak Products | Operational Amplifiers, ADCs, Interface ICs | LiTong
seoDescription: Explore 3peak's comprehensive analog IC portfolio including operational amplifiers, data converters, interface chips, and power management solutions.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by 3peak?
faqs.answer: 3peak offers six core product categories: Operational Amplifiers (precision, high-speed, low-power), ADCs and DACs (12/16/24-bit data converters), Interface Chips (RS485/422/232, CAN bus), Voltage References (high-precision, low-noise), Power Management ICs (LDOs, DC-DC converters), and Motor Drivers. These products serve industrial control, automotive, communications, and consumer markets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our product categories to find the right 3peak components for your analog signal processing needs.

faqs.question: How do I select the right operational amplifier?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right 3peak op-amp depends on your application: Precision applications (sensor conditioning) - choose TP156x series with low offset voltage. High-speed applications (video, communications) - choose TP167x series with high bandwidth. Low-power applications (battery-powered) - choose TP15xx series with microamp quiescent current. Automotive applications - choose AEC-Q100 qualified variants.
faqs.decisionGuide: Precision: TP156x, High-speed: TP167x, Low-power: TP15xx, Automotive: AEC-Q100 variants.

faqs.question: What support does LiTong provide for 3peak products?
faqs.answer: As an authorized 3peak distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive support including product selection guidance, reference design review, SPICE model support, evaluation boards, supply chain management, competitive pricing, and fast delivery. Our FAE team has deep expertise in analog signal chain design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong FAE team for design assistance and product recommendations.

faqs.question: Are 3peak products suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, 3peak offers AEC-Q100 qualified products for automotive applications including op-amps, voltage references, power management ICs, and interface chips. Products feature extended temperature range (-40°C to +125°C) and meet automotive reliability standards. 3peak has passed IATF 16949 certification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Look for AEC-Q100 qualified products for automotive applications.

faqs.question: What is the precision of 3peak ADCs and DACs?
faqs.answer: 3peak offers data converters with various precision levels: 12-bit for general-purpose applications, 16-bit for higher precision requirements, and 24-bit for precision measurement applications. The TPCxxx series offers excellent linearity (INL/DNL) and low noise performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: 12-bit for general, 16-bit for precision, 24-bit for measurement applications.

categories.name: Operational Amplifiers
categories.description: High-performance operational amplifiers including precision, high-speed, and low-power variants for signal conditioning and processing applications.
categories.longDescription: As an authorized 3peak distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive selection and support for 3peak's operational amplifier portfolio. 3peak offers a wide range of op-amps including precision amplifiers with ultra-low offset voltage for sensor signal conditioning, high-speed amplifiers with high slew rate and bandwidth for video and communications applications, and low-power amplifiers with microamp quiescent current for battery-powered devices. All products feature rail-to-rail input/output, wide supply voltage range, and excellent stability.

selectionGuide.title: Operational Amplifier Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: How to choose the right op-amp for your application

faqs.question: What is the difference between TP1561 and TP1562?
faqs.answer: TP1561 is a single-channel precision operational amplifier, while TP1562 is a dual-channel version with two independent op-amps in one package. Both offer similar electrical characteristics: low offset voltage (50μV max), low drift (0.5μV/°C), and rail-to-rail input/output. Choose TP1561 for single-channel applications, TP1562 for dual-channel needs to save board space and cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: TP1561 for single channel, TP1562 for dual channel applications.

faqs.question: What is the maximum supply voltage?
faqs.answer: Most 3peak operational amplifiers support wide supply voltage ranges. TP156x series: 2.5V to 5.5V single supply or ±1.25V to ±2.75V dual supply. TP167x high-speed series: 4.5V to 12V single supply. TP15xx low-power series: 1.8V to 5.5V. Check specific datasheet for exact specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: TP156x: 5.5V max, TP167x: 12V max, TP15xx: 5.5V max.

faqs.question: How do I select the right op-amp for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right op-amp depends on your application requirements: For precision DC applications (sensor conditioning, instrumentation), choose TP156x series with low offset voltage (50μV). For high-speed AC applications (video, communications), choose TP167x series with high bandwidth (100MHz). For battery-powered applications, choose TP15xx series with low quiescent current (10μA). Consider supply voltage, input/output range, and package requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Precision: TP156x, High-speed: TP167x, Low-power: TP15xx.

faqs.question: What is rail-to-rail input/output and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Rail-to-rail input means the op-amp can accept input signals close to both supply rails. Rail-to-rail output means the output can swing close to both supply rails. This is important for maximizing dynamic range, especially in low-voltage single-supply applications. 3peak TP156x series offers true rail-to-rail input and output, allowing full utilization of the supply voltage range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Rail-to-rail is essential for low-voltage single-supply applications to maximize signal range.

faqs.question: Are 3peak op-amps available in automotive grade?
faqs.answer: Yes, many 3peak operational amplifiers are available in AEC-Q100 qualified automotive grades. These devices undergo additional testing and qualification to meet automotive reliability requirements. They support extended temperature range (-40°C to +125°C) and are suitable for under-hood and safety-critical applications. Look for -Q1 suffix or contact LiTong for automotive grade availability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Look for AEC-Q100 qualified versions for automotive applications. Contact FAE for specific part numbers.

products.name: Precision Operational Amplifier
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low offset precision op-amp with 50μV max offset, rail-to-rail I/O, ideal for sensor signal conditioning.
descriptionParagraphs: The TP1561 is a high-precision operational amplifier featuring ultra-low offset voltage of 50μV maximum.
With rail-to-rail input and output, low noise, and excellent stability, it is ideal for precision sensor signal conditioning and instrumentation applications.
The device operates from 2.5V to 5.5V single supply and offers low quiescent current for power-sensitive applications.

faeReview.summary: The TP1561 is an excellent precision op-amp that competes directly with industry standards like TI's OPAx189 and ADI's ADA4522. In my experience, it delivers consistent performance for precision measurement applications.
cons: SOT23 package can be challenging for hand soldering
faeReview.recommendation: Highly recommended for precision DC and low-frequency applications. Use TP167x series for higher bandwidth requirements.

comparison.use_case: TP1562 for dual channel, TP1561 for single channel

companionParts.relationship: Precision voltage reference for sensor excitation
companionParts.notes: Use with TP1561 for complete sensor interface

faqs.question: What is the typical input offset voltage?
faqs.answer: The TP1561 features a maximum input offset voltage of 50μV at 25°C, with typical values around 20μV. The offset voltage drift is 0.5μV/°C maximum. This excellent precision makes it suitable for high-accuracy measurement applications where DC accuracy is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: 50μV max offset is excellent for precision applications. Consider chopper-stabilized amps for even lower offset.

faqs.question: Can it drive capacitive loads?
faqs.answer: The TP1561 can drive capacitive loads up to 1000pF with good stability. For larger capacitive loads, use an isolation resistor (typically 50-100Ω) in series with the output. The device is internally compensated and stable in unity-gain configurations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Stable with capacitive loads up to 1000pF. Use isolation resistor for larger loads.

faqs.question: What is the gain bandwidth product?
faqs.answer: The TP1561 has a gain bandwidth product (GBW) of 2.5MHz. This means at a closed-loop gain of 1, bandwidth is 2.5MHz. At gain of 10, bandwidth is approximately 250kHz. At gain of 100, bandwidth is approximately 25kHz. This GBW is sufficient for most sensor signal conditioning and industrial control applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required GBW as: GBW > Gain × Signal Bandwidth × 10 (margin)

faqs.question: What packages are available for TP1561?
faqs.answer: The TP1561 is available in industry-standard packages: SOT23-5 (small footprint for space-constrained designs), SOIC-8 (easy to prototype and solder). Both packages are RoHS compliant and suitable for industrial temperature range (-40°C to +125°C). The SOT23-5 is ideal for compact designs, while SOIC-8 is preferred for prototyping and hand soldering.
faqs.decisionGuide: SOT23-5 for compact designs, SOIC-8 for prototyping.

faqs.question: How does TP1561 compare to TI OPA189?
faqs.answer: TP1561 offers comparable performance to TI OPA189: Offset voltage (50μV vs 50μV), Bandwidth (2.5MHz vs 3MHz), Slew rate (1.5V/μs vs 2V/μs). The TP1561 provides equivalent DC precision at a lower cost with better supply availability. Both are suitable for precision instrumentation applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: TP1561 offers equivalent performance to OPA189 at better price and availability.

faqs.question: What is the input bias current?
faqs.answer: The TP1561 features CMOS input stage with very low input bias current of typically 1pA at room temperature. This is important for high-impedance sensor applications where bias current can create offset errors. The low bias current makes TP1561 ideal for pH sensors, photodiode amplifiers, and other high-impedance sources.
faqs.decisionGuide: 1pA bias current excellent for high-impedance sensors. No offset from bias current in most applications.

products.name: Dual Precision Operational Amplifier
products.shortDescription: Dual-channel precision op-amp with 50μV offset, rail-to-rail I/O, space-saving solution for multi-channel designs.
descriptionParagraphs: The TP1562 is a dual-channel version of the popular TP1561 precision operational amplifier.
It provides two independent high-precision op-amps in a single package, saving board space and reducing cost for multi-channel applications.
With the same excellent electrical characteristics as TP1561, it is ideal for differential amplifiers, active filters, and multi-sensor systems.
features: Dual precision op-amps in one package

faeReview.summary: The TP1562 offers excellent value for multi-channel precision applications. The dual configuration saves significant board space compared to using two single op-amps.
cons: Limited flexibility compared to discrete op-amps
faeReview.recommendation: Recommended for any application requiring two precision op-amps. The space and cost savings are significant.

comparison.use_case: TP1561 for single channel, TP1562 for dual channel

companionParts.notes: Use with TP1562 for 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier
Follow TP1562 with TPC1610 for complete signal chain
Provides stable reference for bridge sensor excitation

faqs.question: How good is the channel matching?
faqs.answer: The TP1562 offers excellent channel-to-channel matching with typical offset voltage matching of 100μV and drift matching of 1μV/°C. This makes it ideal for differential amplifier configurations where matching is critical for common-mode rejection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Good channel matching for differential applications. Use in instrumentation amplifier configurations.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of dual op-amp vs two single op-amps?
faqs.answer: The TP1562 dual op-amp offers several advantages over using two single TP1561s: Reduced board space (one SOIC-8 vs two SOT23-5s), Lower cost (typically 20-30% savings), Better channel matching (fabricated on same die), Guaranteed thermal tracking (both channels at same temperature), Simplified BOM and inventory. The trade-off is less flexibility in placement and shared power supply.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TP1562 for dual-channel designs to save space and cost. Use TP1561 for single-channel or distributed designs.

faqs.question: Can I use TP1562 for active filter designs?
faqs.answer: Yes, TP1562 is excellent for active filter designs. Both channels can be used to create second-order filters (Sallen-Key, multiple feedback topologies). The 2.5MHz bandwidth supports filters up to about 200kHz. The low offset ensures minimal DC error, and the rail-to-rail output maximizes dynamic range. One package can implement a complete second-order filter or two first-order filters.
faqs.decisionGuide: One TP1562 can implement a complete second-order active filter. Good for anti-aliasing and signal conditioning.

faqs.question: What is crosstalk between channels?
faqs.answer: The TP1562 features excellent channel isolation with crosstalk typically better than -80dB at 1kHz. This means a 1V signal on one channel creates less than 0.1mV interference on the other channel. The high isolation ensures independent operation of both amplifiers, making TP1562 suitable for sensitive multi-channel applications like instrumentation amplifiers.
faqs.decisionGuide: -80dB crosstalk excellent for most applications. Channels are well isolated.

faqs.question: How do I configure TP1562 as an instrumentation amplifier?
faqs.answer: A 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier can be built using two TP1562s (4 op-amps total) or one TP1562 plus one TP1561. Configuration: First stage - two op-amps as differential input buffers with gain, Second stage - one op-amp as differential amplifier. The high CMRR of TP1562 (110dB) provides excellent common-mode rejection. Use precision resistors (0.1%) for best CMRR.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use two TP1562s or TP1562+TP1561 for 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier.

categories.name: ADCs and DACs
categories.description: High-precision analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog converters with 12/16/24-bit resolution for measurement and control applications.
categories.longDescription: As an authorized 3peak distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive selection and support for 3peak's data converter portfolio. 3peak offers high-performance ADCs and DACs with resolutions from 12-bit to 24-bit, featuring excellent linearity, low noise, and high conversion speeds. These converters are ideal for industrial measurement, sensor interfaces, audio processing, and precision control applications.

selectionGuide.title: Data Converter Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: How to choose the right ADC or DAC for your application

faqs.question: What resolution do I need?
faqs.answer: Resolution selection depends on your application: 12-bit ADCs/DACs provide 4096 steps (0.024% resolution) suitable for general-purpose applications. 16-bit provides 65536 steps (0.0015% resolution) for precision measurement. 24-bit provides 16777216 steps (0.000006% resolution) for high-precision measurement and audio. Consider the signal-to-noise ratio and effective number of bits (ENOB) in your selection.
faqs.decisionGuide: 12-bit for general, 16-bit for precision, 24-bit for high-precision applications.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SAR and Delta-Sigma ADCs?
faqs.answer: SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADCs like TPC1610 offer fast conversion speeds (1MSPS) with low latency, making them ideal for multiplexed systems and control loops. Delta-Sigma ADCs offer higher resolution (24-bit) but lower speed and higher latency, better for precision measurement applications. Choose SAR ADCs when speed is critical, Delta-Sigma when highest resolution is needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: SAR for speed and low latency. Delta-Sigma for highest resolution.

faqs.question: How do I choose the right voltage reference for my ADC?
faqs.answer: The voltage reference determines the ADC's input range and LSB size. For a 16-bit ADC with 2.5V reference, LSB = 2.5V/65536 = 38μV. Choose reference based on: Input signal range, Required LSB size, Temperature stability (drift), Initial accuracy. 3peak TPR1025 provides 2.5V with 0.1% accuracy and 10ppm/°C drift, ideal for precision applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match reference to input range. Consider accuracy and drift requirements.

faqs.question: What is ENOB and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: ENOB (Effective Number of Bits) represents the actual dynamic performance of an ADC considering noise and distortion. While TPC1610 is a 16-bit ADC, its ENOB is typically 14.5 bits at 1MSPS due to noise. ENOB decreases at higher input frequencies. For DC measurements, full resolution is available. ENOB matters for AC applications where dynamic performance is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: ENOB is important for AC applications. For DC measurements, focus on INL and offset.

faqs.question: How do I interface the ADC with my microcontroller?
faqs.answer: 3peak ADCs like TPC1610 use SPI-compatible serial interface. Connection requires: SCLK (serial clock), SDI (data input for config), SDO (data output), CS (chip select). Most microcontrollers have dedicated SPI peripherals. Ensure proper timing: Setup/hold times per datasheet, SCLK frequency within spec, Proper CS assertion. 3peak provides reference code for popular MCUs including STM32 and ESP32.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI interface. Check timing requirements in datasheet. Reference code available.

products.name: 16-Bit Precision ADC
products.shortDescription: High-precision 16-bit SAR ADC with 1MSPS sample rate, SPI interface, excellent linearity for industrial measurement.
descriptionParagraphs: The TPC1610 is a high-performance 16-bit successive approximation register (SAR) analog-to-digital converter.
With a maximum sample rate of 1MSPS, excellent linearity (±1 LSB INL), and low power consumption, it is ideal for industrial control and measurement applications.
The device features a SPI-compatible serial interface and operates from a single 2.7V to 5.25V supply.

faeReview.summary: The TPC1610 delivers excellent 16-bit performance at a competitive price point. It is a direct competitor to ADI's AD7980 and TI's ADS8319 with comparable specifications.
faeReview.recommendation: Recommended for industrial measurement and data acquisition applications requiring 16-bit precision at moderate speeds.

comparison.use_case: TPC1210 for cost-sensitive general applications, TPC1610 for precision

companionParts.relationship: Precision 2.5V voltage reference for ADC reference input
companionParts.notes: Use precision reference for best ADC accuracy

companionParts.relationship: Precision op-amp for input signal conditioning
companionParts.notes: Buffer and amplify sensor signals before ADC input
Condition multiple channels before multiplexing to ADC

faqs.question: What is the effective number of bits (ENOB)?
faqs.answer: The TPC1610 achieves approximately 14.5 effective bits (ENOB) at 1MSPS sample rate with a 1kHz input signal. The ENOB represents the actual dynamic performance considering noise and distortion. For DC measurements, the full 16-bit resolution is available.
faqs.decisionGuide: 14.5 ENOB at 1MSPS is good for most applications. Reduce sample rate for better dynamic performance.

faqs.question: What is the SPI interface timing?
faqs.answer: The TPC1610 uses SPI Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0). Key timing parameters: SCLK frequency up to 50MHz, CS setup time 10ns min, CS hold time 10ns min, Data setup time 5ns min, Data hold time 5ns min. Conversion is initiated on CS falling edge, and data is clocked out on SCLK rising edge. Total conversion time is approximately 1μs at 1MSPS.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI Mode 0. Ensure SCLK frequency and timing meet datasheet requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the LSB size?
faqs.answer: LSB (Least Significant Bit) size = Vref / 2^16. For TPC1610 with 2.5V reference: LSB = 2.5V / 65536 = 38.15μV. This means each code change represents approximately 38μV. For 5V reference: LSB = 76.3μV. Understanding LSB helps determine if the ADC resolution is sufficient for your measurement requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate LSB based on your reference voltage. Ensure it's smaller than your required measurement resolution.

faqs.question: What input driver amplifier should I use?
faqs.answer: The TPC1610 requires a driver amplifier that can settle to 16-bit accuracy within the acquisition time. Recommended: TP1561 for precision low-speed signals, TP167x for high-speed signals. Key requirements: Sufficient bandwidth (>10x sample rate), Low output impedance, Fast settling time. The driver must charge the ADC sampling capacitor (typically 20pF) to 0.0015% accuracy (1/2 LSB) within the acquisition window.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TP1561 for most applications. Ensure driver has sufficient bandwidth and settling time.

faqs.question: Can I use TPC1610 in a multiplexed system?
faqs.answer: Yes, TPC1610 is well-suited for multiplexed systems due to its fast conversion time (1μs) and low latency. When multiplexing: Allow adequate settling time after channel switching (typically 100-500ns), Use appropriate anti-aliasing filters for each channel, Consider using a buffer amplifier per channel or a fast-settling mux amplifier. Maximum aggregate throughput depends on number of channels and required sample rate per channel.
faqs.decisionGuide: Excellent for multiplexed systems. Allow settling time between channel switches.

categories.name: Interface Chips
categories.description: Robust interface transceivers for RS-485/422/232, CAN bus, and I2C level shifting applications in industrial and automotive environments.
categories.longDescription: As an authorized 3peak distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive selection and support for 3peak's interface chip portfolio. 3peak offers robust transceivers for industrial and automotive communication standards including RS-485/RS-422, RS-232, CAN bus, and I2C level shifters. These devices feature high ESD protection, wide common-mode range, and fault-tolerant operation for harsh environments.

selectionGuide.title: Interface Chip Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: How to choose the right interface transceiver for your application

faqs.question: What is the difference between RS-485 and RS-422?
faqs.answer: RS-485 supports multi-point (up to 32 nodes) half-duplex or full-duplex communication with differential signaling. RS-422 is point-to-point (1 driver, up to 10 receivers) full-duplex only. Both use differential signaling for noise immunity. RS-485 is more common for industrial networks, while RS-422 is used for longer point-to-point links. 3peak offers transceivers for both standards.
faqs.decisionGuide: RS-485 for multi-node networks, RS-422 for point-to-point. Check specific product for half/full duplex support.

faqs.question: How many nodes can be connected to an RS-485 bus?
faqs.answer: Standard RS-485 allows 32 unit loads (32 nodes with standard receivers). The TPT485 features 1/8 unit load receiver input impedance, allowing up to 256 transceivers on the same bus. For even larger networks, use repeaters to extend the bus. Each repeater creates a new bus segment supporting another 256 nodes.
faqs.decisionGuide: 256 nodes max with TPT485. Use repeaters for larger networks or longer distances.

faqs.question: What is the maximum cable length for RS-485 communication?
faqs.answer: Maximum cable length depends on data rate: At 100kbps: up to 1200m, At 1Mbps: up to 100m, At 10Mbps: up to 10m. Use twisted-pair cable (Cat5e or similar) for best noise immunity. Terminate both ends of the bus with 120Ω resistors. Use proper grounding to avoid ground loops. For longer distances, use repeaters or reduce data rate.
faqs.decisionGuide: Shorter distance at higher speeds. Use twisted pair and proper termination.

faqs.question: What is fail-safe operation in RS-485 transceivers?
faqs.answer: Fail-safe ensures defined receiver output when the bus is idle, open, or shorted. Without fail-safe, noise can cause false triggering. TPT485 includes integrated fail-safe that forces receiver output high (logic 1) for: Open bus (no active driver), Shorted bus (A=B), Idle bus (no differential voltage). This eliminates the need for external biasing resistors.
faqs.decisionGuide: TPT485 has internal fail-safe. No external biasing resistors needed.

faqs.question: How do I protect my RS-485 interface from ESD and transients?
faqs.answer: The TPT485 features ±18kV HBM ESD protection on bus pins, sufficient for most applications. For additional protection in harsh environments: Use TVS diodes on bus lines, Add series resistors to limit current, Use isolated transceivers for high-voltage environments, Implement proper PCB layout with guard rings. 3peak also offers higher protection variants for extreme environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: TPT485 has 18kV ESD protection built-in. Add TVS diodes for extreme environments.

products.name: RS-485/RS-422 Transceiver
products.shortDescription: High-speed RS-485/RS-422 transceiver with 20Mbps data rate, ±18kV ESD protection, 1/8 unit load for up to 256 nodes.
descriptionParagraphs: The TPT485 is a robust RS-485/RS-422 transceiver designed for harsh industrial environments.
It supports data rates up to 20Mbps and features ±18kV HBM ESD protection on the bus pins.
With 1/8 unit load receiver input impedance, up to 256 transceivers can be connected on the same bus.

faeReview.summary: The TPT485 is a solid RS-485 transceiver that competes well with industry standards like MAX485 and SN75176. The high ESD protection and wide common-mode range make it suitable for demanding industrial applications.
faeReview.recommendation: Recommended for industrial RS-485 networks requiring high reliability and ESD protection.

comparison.use_case: MAX485 for basic applications, TPT485 for high-performance
SN75176 for legacy designs, TPT485 for new designs

companionParts.relationship: Precision op-amp for analog signal conditioning
companionParts.notes: Condition analog signals before digitization and transmission
Digitize analog sensor data before RS-485 transmission
Provide stable reference for sensor excitation and ADC

faqs.question: How many nodes can be on the bus?
faqs.answer: The TPT485 features 1/8 unit load receiver input impedance, allowing up to 256 transceivers on the same bus (standard RS-485 allows 32 unit loads). This is useful for large industrial networks. For even larger networks, use repeaters to extend the bus.
faqs.decisionGuide: 256 nodes max with TPT485. Use repeaters for larger networks or longer distances.

faqs.question: What is the maximum data rate and cable length?
faqs.answer: The TPT485 supports data rates up to 20Mbps. Maximum cable length depends on data rate: At 100kbps: up to 1200m, At 1Mbps: up to 100m, At 10Mbps: up to 10m, At 20Mbps: up to 5m. Use twisted-pair cable (Cat5e or better) for best noise immunity. Higher data rates require shorter cables due to signal attenuation and timing constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: 20Mbps max. Reduce cable length at higher speeds. Use twisted pair cable.

faqs.question: How do I terminate an RS-485 bus properly?
faqs.answer: Proper termination is critical for reliable RS-485 operation: Place 120Ω termination resistors at both ends of the bus only, Do not place termination at intermediate nodes, Use resistors matched to cable characteristic impedance (typically 100-120Ω), For long buses or high speeds, termination is essential to prevent reflections. The TPT485 does not include internal termination - external resistors required.
faqs.decisionGuide: 120Ω resistors at both ends only. Match to cable impedance.

faqs.question: What is the common-mode voltage range?
faqs.answer: The TPT485 supports a wide common-mode voltage range of -7V to +12V. This means the receiver can correctly interpret differential signals even when both A and B lines are shifted by up to -7V or +12V relative to ground. This wide range is essential for reliable operation in industrial environments with ground potential differences between nodes.
faqs.decisionGuide: -7V to +12V common-mode range handles most industrial ground differential scenarios.

faqs.question: How do I protect against ESD and surges?
faqs.answer: The TPT485 includes ±18kV HBM ESD protection on bus pins, sufficient for most applications. For additional protection in harsh environments: Use TVS diodes (e.g., SMBJ series) on bus lines, Add series resistors (10-47Ω) to limit current, Use isolated transceivers for high-voltage environments, Implement proper PCB layout with guard rings and proper grounding. For outdoor or extreme environments, consider additional surge protection devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: 18kV ESD built-in. Add TVS diodes for extreme environments.

categories.name: Power Management ICs
categories.description: High-performance voltage references, LDO regulators, and power management solutions for precision analog and digital systems.
categories.longDescription: As an authorized 3peak distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive selection and support for 3peak's power management portfolio. 3peak offers high-precision voltage references with excellent initial accuracy and low drift, low-dropout (LDO) linear regulators with high PSRR and low noise, and comprehensive power management solutions for analog and mixed-signal systems.

selectionGuide.title: Power Management Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: How to choose the right voltage reference or LDO for your application

faqs.question: What is the difference between voltage reference and LDO?
faqs.answer: A voltage reference is designed for precision and stability, providing an accurate voltage for ADC/DAC references and sensor excitation. It prioritizes accuracy and drift over current drive. An LDO (Low Dropout Regulator) is designed to provide clean power to circuits, prioritizing PSRR (power supply rejection), low noise, and current drive capability. Use voltage references for precision reference applications, LDOs for powering circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Voltage reference for ADC/DAC reference inputs. LDO for powering analog circuits.

faqs.question: How do I choose the right voltage reference for my application?
faqs.answer: Key factors for voltage reference selection: Initial accuracy (0.1% for TPR1025), Temperature drift (10ppm/°C for TPR1025), Output noise (4μVp-p for TPR1025), Load regulation, Line regulation. For high-precision ADCs, choose references with low drift and low noise. For battery applications, consider quiescent current. 3peak offers 2.5V, 3.0V, 4.096V, and 5.0V reference options.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider accuracy, drift, noise for precision. Contact FAE for specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is temperature drift and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Temperature drift (ppm/°C) measures how much the reference voltage changes with temperature. For TPR1025 with 10ppm/°C drift: Over -40°C to +85°C range (125°C span), voltage changes by 1250ppm or 0.125%. This is critical for precision applications where accuracy must be maintained across temperature. Lower drift means better stability. 3peak offers 5ppm/°C, 10ppm/°C, and 20ppm/°C options.
faqs.decisionGuide: Lower drift is better for precision. 10ppm/°C suitable for most industrial applications.

faqs.question: Do I need an output capacitor for the voltage reference?
faqs.answer: The TPR1025 is designed to be stable without an external output capacitor, saving board space and BOM cost. However, for applications with transient load currents or long output traces, a 0.1μF to 1μF ceramic capacitor can be added for additional stability. The device is stable with any capacitive load. For ADC reference inputs, follow ADC datasheet recommendations for decoupling.
faqs.decisionGuide: No capacitor required for normal operation. Add 0.1-1μF for long traces or transient loads.

faqs.question: What output voltages are available from 3peak voltage references?
faqs.answer: 3peak offers voltage references in standard output voltages: 2.5V (TPR1025) - most common for ADC references, 3.0V - for 3.3V system references, 4.096V - provides 1mV/LSB for 12-bit ADCs, 5.0V - for 5V system references. All voltages are available in various accuracy and drift grades. Contact LiTong for specific part numbers and availability.
faqs.decisionGuide: 2.5V most common. 4.096V convenient for 12-bit systems. Match to your ADC requirements.

products.name: Precision Voltage Reference
products.shortDescription: High-precision 2.5V voltage reference with 0.1% initial accuracy, 10ppm/°C drift, low noise for precision measurement.
descriptionParagraphs: The TPR1025 is a high-precision 2.5V voltage reference designed for precision measurement and data conversion applications.
It offers excellent initial accuracy of 0.1% and low temperature drift of 10ppm/°C, ensuring stable reference voltage over temperature and time.
With low output noise and good line/load regulation, it is ideal for ADC/DAC reference inputs and sensor excitation.

faeReview.summary: The TPR1025 offers excellent precision at a competitive price. It compares favorably with ADI's ADR4525 and TI's REF5025 for many applications.
cons: Lower drive current than some competitors
faeReview.recommendation: Recommended for precision reference applications requiring 2.5V output. Use with TPC1610 ADC for best performance.

comparison.use_case: ADR4525 for ultra-precision, TPR1025 for cost-effective precision
REF5025 for higher precision, TPR1025 for general precision

companionParts.notes: TPR1025 provides stable reference for TPC1610 ADC
Use with TPR1025 for complete sensor interface
Condition multiple channels using same reference

faqs.question: Do I need an output capacitor?
faqs.answer: The TPR1025 is designed to be stable without an external output capacitor, which saves board space and BOM cost. However, for applications with transient load currents or long output traces, a 0.1μF to 1μF ceramic capacitor can be added for additional stability. The device is stable with any capacitive load.
faqs.decisionGuide: No capacitor required for normal operation. Add 0.1-1μF for long traces or transient loads.

faqs.question: What is the load regulation specification?
faqs.answer: The TPR1025 features excellent load regulation of 50ppm/mA typical. This means the output voltage changes by only 50 microvolts per milliamp of load current change. For a 2.5V reference, this is 125μV/mA. Good load regulation ensures stable reference voltage even as ADC reference input current varies during conversion.
faqs.decisionGuide: 50ppm/mA load regulation excellent for ADC references. Ensure adequate drive capability for your load.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect reference accuracy?
faqs.answer: Temperature affects reference voltage through the temperature coefficient (drift). TPR1025 has 10ppm/°C max drift. Over industrial temperature range (-40°C to +85°C, 125°C span): Total drift = 10ppm/°C × 125°C = 1250ppm = 0.125%. For 2.5V reference, this is ±3.125mV variation. Combined with initial accuracy (0.1%), total error is approximately 0.225% over temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total error from initial accuracy plus temperature drift. 10ppm/°C suitable for most applications.

faqs.question: What is the noise performance?
faqs.answer: The TPR1025 features low output noise of 4μVp-p (0.1Hz to 10Hz bandwidth). This low-frequency noise is important for DC measurement applications. For wideband noise, the spectral density is typically 50nV/√Hz. Low noise is critical for high-resolution ADCs where reference noise can limit overall system performance. For 16-bit ADC with 2.5V reference, 1 LSB = 38μV, so reference noise should be well below this.
faqs.decisionGuide: 4μVp-p noise excellent for 16-bit ADCs. Noise well below 1 LSB for TPC1610.

faqs.question: Can I use TPR1025 for ratiometric measurements?
faqs.answer: Yes, TPR1025 is excellent for ratiometric measurement applications. In ratiometric configurations, the same reference voltage excites the sensor and provides ADC reference. This cancels out reference voltage errors since both sensor output and ADC range scale together. Common applications: Bridge sensors (load cells, pressure sensors), RTD measurements, Potentiometric position sensors. Use TPR1025 for both sensor excitation and ADC reference.
faqs.decisionGuide: Excellent for ratiometric measurements. Use for bridge sensors and RTD applications.
